artifacts.content:
Hey Mom,

Finally sitting down for a minute, so I figured I’d actually write instead of just sending you random photos of muddy horses.

I was thinking about how long Amanda and I have really been in each other’s lives. Technically I’ve known her since March 2019, when Dr. Ellison at Longs Peak Behavioral Health nudged her into weekly lessons with me at Blue Sage as part of her PTSD plan after the Willow Creek barn fire on County Road 21 where she lost that gelding. It feels like a lifetime ago. Around the same time, early 2019, Dad co‑signed that $6,800 loan so I could buy my first OTTB and move him into that boarding barn on CR 19. I was still working off board and riding problem horses for the owner after my Fort Collins schooling show concussion the previous fall.

Funny how those few months spun out into everything now: Blue Sage, the mustangs, FrontRange letting me work weird hours. I still keep that Thursday afternoon “lesson block” in my head from when I came back from the 3‑month medical leave for anxiety in spring 2019 and Elena at FrontRange approved the reduced schedule so I could get to the barn. It’s basically my mental anchor, even if the actual time has shifted.

Anyway, how are you and Dad? How’s he feeling since January? I’ll be up Sunday for dinner unless the weather goes totally sideways and the roads ice over. I can swing by Loveland first if you want help with groceries or snow shoveling.

Love you,
Jess

Journal – 03/18/2024

Rough couple of weeks. Ranger’s mild colic episode reminded me how thin I’m running the line between “dedicated” and “burned out.” I was up past midnight three nights in a row here at the house on CR 19, walking him, checking gut sounds, texting vitals to the on‑call vet, then turning around and logging into FrontRange by 7:00 a.m. for Zooms with Elena. My therapist at Longs Peak Counseling Center was pretty blunt last session: if I keep doing midnight barn checks plus 7 a.m. calls, the stress migraines are probably coming back.

It’s not the first time my work and horses have collided. Back in fall 2022, Daniel put me on that performance improvement plan on August 29 because I missed two internal reporting deadlines while I was hand‑walking that sore client gelding every two hours for three days. He wasn’t wrong about the deadlines, but the vet had been clear about the risk if we didn’t keep him moving.

Same pattern with money: October 2023 I floated about $2,000 of feed and vet on my Elevations credit card for Mark Ellison’s bay mare because his wage garnishment left him three months behind on the $650/month training board. I keep saying my “concern” is always the horses, but the truth is I’m starting to worry about me too.

Goal for April: hard boundaries. No more all‑night checks unless it’s life‑or‑death, no more fronting months of expenses for clients without a plan. Horses are fine. I need to be, too.

Hi Elena,

I wanted to give you a heads‑up about the next couple of weeks and reassure you that project timelines are still on track.

Evan is scheduled for outpatient surgery at Longmont United Hospital on January 11th to remove a benign testicular mass. I’ll be at Blue Sage by 5:30 a.m. that morning to ride two client horses and hand‑walk a rehab gelding, then I’ll drive him to his 9:00 a.m. check‑in and stay through the approximately 2‑hour procedure. Assuming everything goes as planned, I’ll be online from the hospital Wi‑Fi by 1:00 p.m. and can cover my usual meetings that afternoon.

This isn’t the first time I’ve had to juggle family and work like this—when my younger brother Ryan had that serious depressive episode last March and spent three days in the behavioral health unit at Poudre Valley Hospital in Fort Collins, I still did my 6:00–9:00 a.m. colt sessions at Blue Sage, put in a full 8‑hour remote day for FrontRange, then drove up to sit with him and talk to his psychiatrist, Dr. Keller, until visiting hours ended at 9:00 p.m. four nights in a row. It was a lot, but we kept the projects moving.

For this upcoming stretch, I’ve front‑loaded analysis work and moved any non‑urgent internal meetings off Evan’s surgery day. If something unexpected comes up clinically, I may need to step away briefly, but I don’t anticipate missing any deadlines.

Thanks for the flexibility,
Jessica

Event: Evening barn time / de-stress
Date: 2024-08-15
Time: 4:00 p.m. – 7:30 p.m.
Location: Blue Sage Equestrian Center, Berthoud
Attendees: Jessica

Notes:

Try to keep this as an actual buffer, not “extra work.” Plan:
- 4:00–4:45 – Ride Juniper (focus on lateral work, keep it quiet)
- 4:45–5:30 – Watch Amanda school her gelding, maybe swap rides
- 5:30–6:15 – Light groundwork with anxious bay mustang mare (the one that had the bad experience with previous trainer). Keep sessions under 20 minutes; don’t repeat the late January pattern of four straight hours in the wind after Ryan’s DUI sentencing hearing in Fort Collins.
- 6:15–7:00 – Help 4‑H kids if they’re around; otherwise hand‑walk rehab gelding.

Reminders to self:
- Since Dad’s minor stroke on January 12, 2024, I’ve been doing fewer but longer evenings at the barn—3 nights a week, 2–3 hours. That’s okay, but call Mom from the lounge instead of pacing the rail the whole time.
- Keep an eye on Ryan; he’s been off since the DUI hearing in Larimer County last June. If he comes along, let him sit in the lounge instead of hovering in the arena.
- No driving home after 10:30 p.m. again like last summer when Evan was on 12‑hour night shifts at Poudre and there’d just been that minor break‑in on County Road 19.

This block is for breathing, not adding more to the to‑do list.

metadata.title: Evening barn time / de-stress
metadata.location: Blue Sage Equestrian Center, Berthoud, CO

artifacts.content:
Hey Amanda,

I was updating my little training notebook and realized just how long we’ve been orbiting the same barns.

Technically, I’ve known “you” since before we knew each other—back in February 2019 when I was at the Larimer County Courthouse for that small‑claims mediation over the Willow Creek boarding contract mess. There was another woman in the hallway dealing with her own Willow Creek dispute; I’m still pretty sure that was you, even though we didn’t connect names until later when we both ended up at the CR 19 barn.

Then there’s your side of it: you’ve told me your therapist at Longs Peak Behavioral Health, Dr. Karen Ellison, first suggested you start weekly lessons with me at Blue Sage in March 2019 after the barn fire on County Road 21 where you lost your gelding. Wild to think that that awful fire and those courthouse hallways are what eventually led to us co‑hosting schooling shows and hauling to Wyoming together.

On my end, spring 2019 was also when I was dragging myself back from that 3‑month medical leave from FrontRange for anxiety and panic attacks. HR (Elena) approved that reduced schedule so I could keep a Thursday afternoon lesson block at Blue Sage as part of my coping routine. I still think of Thursdays as “non‑negotiable barn time” because of that.

Anyway, just feeling weirdly grateful tonight that all those messy pieces—lawsuits, fires, panic attacks—somehow turned into this little community we’ve got. Thanks for being in the middle of it with me.

See you tomorrow for Juniper + your boys,
Jess

metadata.subject: Seven-ish years of chaos and horses

artifacts.content:
Training/Facility Notes – Safety + Setup

I keep reminding myself that the current Blue Sage setup for wild/green horses is the product of a lot of hard lessons.

March 2023: Ranger’s accident in the 40' x 40' pen. Neighbor Keller’s ATV backfired right along the fence line, he panicked, flipped over a panel, and we ended up hauling to CSU Veterinary Teaching Hospital for 12 stitches on his shoulder. Dr. Megan Willis was on that 2.5‑hour emergency call. After that, we upgraded to 6' heavy‑duty panels, smoothed every weld, and rerouted ATV traffic.

July 7, 2022: surprise Larimer County inspection. Ruiz from Animal Control flagged the old perimeter fencing and threatened a $1,500 fine. I basically project‑managed Susan through three weeks of replacing about 300 feet with new 6' no‑climb around the wild horse pens and the 20' round pen. Re‑inspection on July 28, signed off.

April 18, 2022: small grass fire along the east fence from neighbor Dave’s burn pile. Berthoud Fire had it contained in ~30 minutes, but watching embers blow toward the mustang pens was enough. We put in a 15' bare‑earth firebreak along 200 feet of fence and moved wild horse turnout to the gravel dry lot farther from the property line.

May 2020: my mild concussion from the fresh mustang in the 60' round pen. ER at Longs Peak, then new rule: no solo sessions with new mustangs; second competent person on‑site; added quick‑release tie rings at wash rack and high‑tie.

Bottom line: the facilities are “good” because we’ve already paid the price of finding the weak spots. Don’t forget that.

Hi Dad,

I was driving down Highway 287 past Callahan’s place yesterday and thought about that broncy mustang mare he tried to send me in September 2022. Remember how he offered $1,200 for a 30‑day “tune‑up” so his 12‑year‑old granddaughter could lope her around, even though the mare had already put a rider in the ER at UCHealth Medical Center of the Rockies in July? Saying no to that job, with $900 of Cooper’s vet bills sitting on my credit card, is still one of the clearest examples I have of choosing my values over quick cash.

Same with Cooper himself back in early 2019—after that Longmont mobile clinic over‑sedated him for dental work and he panicked coming out of it, rearing and striking whenever he saw a syringe. I worked him 4–5 days a week here in Berthoud, 10–15 minute chunks, until by May 2019 he’d stand quietly for vaccines again. That’s the kind of “difficult” horse I’m okay putting my name on.

It’s funny to think it all traces back to late 2018 when you first had me out to the place on County Road 19 to restart that mare after my own hauling wreck near Cheyenne and the broken wrist and the stupid trailer insurance mess Mom still grumbles about. Somewhere between that and Ranger’s nylon halter accident at the Larimer County Fairgrounds in August 2022—and those 90 days I spent getting him from “auction candidate” to walk‑trot on the track—you and Mom started trusting that I actually know what I’m doing.

Thanks again for co‑signing that first $6,800 OTTB loan and for still picking up the phone when I call about money or horses.

Love,
Jess

From: Jessica Marie Thompson <j.thompson@frontrangehealth.com>
To: Amanda Leigh Rodriguez <arodriguez@bluesageeq.com>
Date: 2023-05-12
Subject: Why I’m basically living at the barn (and why I keep sending people your way too)

Hey Amanda,

Figured I’d write this down because the last year or so has been a blur of horses and hospital Zoom calls.

Since my panic attack at work on March 3, 2023, my therapist, Dr. Elaine Porter at Front Range Behavioral Health, actually put “barn time” into my mental health treatment plan. From March through May 2023 I’ve been at Blue Sage Equestrian Center in Berthoud almost every single afternoon—5–6 days per week—just breathing, watching you and me and everyone else ride. Half the time I park on the rail after my remote shift with FrontRange Health Analytics and watch Juniper go, or whatever green client horse we’ve got in.

Thinking about it, that’s exactly why I recommend you so strongly when people ask about unbroken or difficult horses. After my wreck in May 2021 at the Blue Sage arena—the one where I got the concussion and then froze up and started over-correcting my 3‑year‑old Quarter Horse mare—you were the one who quietly restarted both of us. I hauled her to your place outside Berthoud three evenings a week in June and July 2021 after my FrontRange hours. You had us do slow groundwork, short rides, and super clear, step‑by‑step instructions so I wouldn’t spiral when she got tight. By August 2021 we were back to trailering to those little Loveland shows without panic attacks.

Anyway, just wanted you to know that between the therapy plan, the barn time, and your patience, I’m finally feeling like myself again.

Jess

metadata.subject: Why I’m basically living at the barn

artifacts.content:
From: Jessica Marie Thompson <jess.thompson@gmail.com>
To: Mark Allen Thompson <mark.thompson.fc@gmail.com>
Date: 2021-10-06
Subject: Moving the mustangs + why I keep pointing people to Blue Sage

Hey Dad,

I wanted to follow up about the situation at 2149 County Road 19. After the neighbor, Carol Jensen, filed that noise complaint with the Larimer County Sheriff’s Office Code Enforcement Unit on October 3, 2021, about the late‑night trailer loading, I’ve been thinking hard. That makes two written warnings about after‑10 p.m. noise tied to those three late‑night trailer loading sessions. I don’t want you or Mom dealing with more hassle.

So I’ve decided to move Ranger and Juniper completely off your property and keep all the wild horse work at Blue Sage Equestrian Center here in Berthoud. The facilities there are just better suited for mustangs anyway—more space between pens and neighboring homes, designated training hours, solid fencing. It’s safer for the horses and less stressful for everyone.

Honestly, that’s also why I keep recommending Blue Sage when people ask if I’d take on their unbroken or difficult horses. Between the setup there and the way we structure things, it’s ideal. Think about Amanda Rodriguez’s mustang mare back in early 2020—the one that got dangerous at that low‑budget boarding barn near Greeley. After the mare charged the gate and knocked Amanda down in March 2020 and she ended up at UCHealth Medical Center of the Rockies in Loveland for stitches and a CT scan, bringing the mare to my arena here in Berthoud in April and committing to 60 days of groundwork made all the difference.

Love you,
Jess

metadata.subject: Moving the mustangs + why I keep pointing people to Blue Sage

artifacts.content:
Apple Notes – Personal log
Date: 2022-08-30
Title: Why I keep saying yes to the hard ones

August has been a reminder of why I do this.

On August 9, 2022, I hauled Emily Sanchez’s little mare to Aspen Meadow Veterinary Clinic for a nasty hoof abscess. Emily’s mom, Carla, pulled me aside in the parking lot and said they were behind on rent at their trailer park in Loveland and couldn’t cover the $300 farrier and vet bill. I ended up paying the invoice with my own debit card. I wrote it off in my head as pro bono/charity—twice a week lessons for the rest of August 2022 at the Larimer County Fairgrounds, free. Emily shows up early, cleans her tack, and listens. The mare’s staying sound and Emily isn’t carrying the guilt of “breaking” her horse.

It all ties back to the “difficult” ones. Amanda’s mustang that charged the gate and knocked her down at that Greeley barn in March 2020, sending her to the ER at UCHealth Medical Center of the Rockies for stitches and a CT scan. The 60 days of groundwork we did after she brought the mare to my arena in April 2020, and the boundary I set that she needed counseling for her fear before I’d put her back on. By July 2020 the mare was leading politely, standing tied, and walking under saddle.

Horses like that—and kids like Emily—are why I keep saying yes, even when it stretches the budget and my energy.

metadata.title: Why I keep saying yes to the hard ones

artifacts.content:
Subject: Why I’ve basically been living at Blue Sage this fall

Hey Evan,

I wanted to write this out instead of just mumbling about it over takeout.

After the written performance warning from FrontRange Health Analytics, Inc. on September 9, 2022, I asked HR to temporarily reduce my hours. I framed it as needing more focus time, but honestly it was also about sanity. Since then, for most of fall 2022—so roughly September through November—I’ve been at Blue Sage Equestrian Center in Berthoud, CO 4–5 days a week in the afternoons.

Some days I’m riding or doing groundwork myself, but a lot of the time I just park on the rail and watch Jessica Marie Thompson work. This fall she’s had that string of three difficult client horses plus her mustang gelding Ranger, and there’s something weirdly calming about seeing her chip away at things session by session. Watching her handle Ranger when he gets tight, or quietly restart a conversation with a horse that wants to leave, has been the best de‑stress routine I’ve found.

I know it means I’m home later and the farmhouse on County Road 19 is mostly just where I sleep and inhale frozen pizza, but the tradeoff for my headspace has been huge. The reduced work hours at FrontRange are scary financially, and the warning was a gut punch, but being at the barn that much has reminded me what good, steady work looks like.

Just wanted you to understand why I keep choosing the arena rail over happy hour.

– A

metadata.subject: Why I’ve basically been living at Blue Sage this fall

artifacts.content:
Subject: Temporary update on riding services (wrist injury)

Hi everyone,

I wanted to reach out personally about a fall I had this week and how it affects our upcoming sessions.

On Tuesday, May 18, 2021, during a lesson in the main Blue Sage Equestrian arena, I misjudged a young mustang’s readiness to canter under saddle. I asked for canter too soon, he panicked, and I came off. I was seen later that afternoon at UCHealth Urgent Care in Longmont, CO, where they confirmed I fractured my left wrist. I’m in a brace for approximately six weeks.

I take full responsibility for pushing that horse one step past where he was truly ready. It’s important to me that you hear that directly: this was my error in judgment, not “just one of those things that happens with green horses.”

Practically, this means I’ll be limiting under‑saddle riding for a bit. During this six‑week period I’ll still be offering groundwork, in‑hand work, and unmounted lessons, and I’m happy to adjust goals so horses keep progressing without compromising safety.

For those of you who had riding sessions booked that I can’t safely complete, I’ll be issuing refunds. In particular, if you had two canceled under‑saddle sessions on the calendar over the next month, you’ll see a $250 refund from Blue Sage Equestrian LLC this week. My training contract technically doesn’t require this, but owning my mistake and keeping trust with you and your horses matters more to me than the short‑term income.

Thank you for your understanding and for trusting me with your horses.

Best,

Jessica Marie Thompson

metadata.subject: Temporary update on riding services (wrist injury)
